items.q: If the distance between two masses doubles, what happens to the gravitational force between them?
items.correct: It quarters — gravitational force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
items.misconception: It halves — gravitational force is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It doubles — more distance means more space for gravity to act through.
items.why: Students default to linear relationships and forget the inverse-square law; they apply 1/r reasoning instead of 1/r^2.

items.q: Why is gravitational potential energy near a planet treated as negative?
items.correct: Because the zero is defined at infinity; objects bound to the planet have lower (negative) potential energy than at infinity.
items.misconception: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity always points downwards.
items.random_wrong: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity does negative work.
items.why: Students confuse the negative sign as 'direction' rather than understanding it as a consequence of choosing infinity as the zero point.

items.q: What does the spacing of field lines on a diagram indicate?
items.correct: The strength of the field — closer lines mean a stronger field.
items.misconception: Field lines are equally important regardless of spacing — only the arrows matter.
items.random_wrong: Wider spacing means a stronger field because the lines have more room to act.
items.why: Students treat field lines as a count rather than a density-based representation; they miss that the convention encodes strength via spacing.

items.q: If the distance between two masses doubles, the gravitational force between them is:
items.why: Correct answer: Quartered. “Halved” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Gravitational potential at infinity is taken to be:
items.why: Correct answer: Zero. “Infinite” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
